questions.questionText: The most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia worldwide is:
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency remains the commonest cause of microcytic anemia globally.

questions.questionText: The most specific laboratory marker of iron deficiency is:
questions.explanation: Low ferritin is the most specific indicator of depleted iron stores in the absence of inflammation.

questions.questionText: A megaloblastic anemia with neurologic deficits is most suggestive of deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastosis with neurologic manifestations due to demyelination.

questions.questionText: The peripheral smear finding classically seen in hereditary spherocytosis is:
questions.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis shows spherocytes with increased osmotic fragility.

questions.questionText: A markedly elevated PT and aPTT with low fibrinogen and high D-dimer is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Disseminated intravascular coagulation causes consumptive coagulopathy and secondary fibrinolysis.

questions.questionText: The most common inherited thrombophilia due to APC resistance is:
questions.explanation: Factor V Leiden is the most prevalent inherited thrombophilia in many populations.

questions.questionText: Auer rods on peripheral smear are classically associated with:
questions.explanation: Auer rods are myeloid inclusions and strongly support a diagnosis of AML.

questions.questionText: The cytogenetic abnormality t(15;17) is seen in:
questions.explanation: Acute promyelocytic leukemia has t(15;17) and responds to ATRA-based therapy.

questions.questionText: The hallmark molecular abnormality in CML is:
questions.explanation: CML is defined by Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) producing BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase.

questions.questionText: Imatinib is a targeted therapy primarily used in:
questions.explanation: Imatinib inhibits BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase and transformed CML outcomes.

questions.questionText: An isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation profile and mucocutaneous bleeding suggests:
questions.optionD: TTP with hemolysis in all cases
questions.explanation: ITP often presents as isolated low platelets with normal PT/aPTT and mucocutaneous bleeding.

questions.questionText: The classic pentad of TTP includes thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia plus:
questions.explanation: TTP classically has MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, renal injury, and fever.

questions.questionText: Sickle cell disease is caused by a point mutation in the β-globin gene resulting in substitution of:
questions.optionA: Lysine for glutamic acid at position 6
questions.optionB: Valine for glutamic acid at position 6 (HbS — GAG→GTG)
questions.optionD: Glycine for glutamic acid at position 26
questions.explanation: Sickle cell disease: AR, point mutation in β-globin gene → valine replaces glutamic acid at position 6 (GAG→GTG). HbS polymerizes under deoxygenation → sickle-shaped RBCs. Complications: vaso-occlusive crises (bone pain), acute chest syndrome, splenic sequestration/autosplenism, stroke, avascular necrosis of femoral head, aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19). Target cells and sickle cells on smear. HbF protective (hydroxyurea increases HbF). Newborn screening: Hb electrophoresis. Reference: Harrison — Sickle Cell Disease.

questions.optionB: Monoclonal plasma cell proliferation in bone marrow with CRAB criteria (Calcium elevated, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions)
questions.explanation: Multiple myeloma: malignant clonal plasma cell neoplasm. CRAB criteria: Calcium elevation (>11 mg/dL), Renal insufficiency (creatinine >2), Anemia (Hb <10), Bone lesions (lytic — punched-out, NO blastic). M-spike on SPEP (monoclonal IgG most common). Bence Jones proteinuria (free light chains). Rouleaux formation on smear. Bone marrow: >10% clonal plasma cells. Complications: pathological fractures, amyloidosis (AL), hyperviscosity. Treatment: bortezomib-based regimens, stem cell transplant (eligible). Reference: Harrison — Multiple Myeloma.

questions.questionText: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) is most commonly triggered by:
questions.optionB: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 (STEC-HUS — bloody diarrhea followed by triad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, AKI)
questions.explanation: Typical HUS (STEC-HUS): most common in children. Triggered by Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 (bloody diarrhea → 5-7 days later → HUS triad). Triad: (1) MAHA (schistocytes, fragmented RBCs), (2) Thrombocytopenia, (3) AKI. Do NOT give antibiotics (may worsen by increasing toxin release). Atypical HUS: complement-mediated (factor H/I mutations) — treat with eculizumab (anti-C5 mAb). TTP: similar but neurologic predominance, ADAMTS13 deficiency. Reference: Harrison — HUS.

questions.questionText: Hemophilia A is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Factor VIII (X-linked recessive — elevated aPTT with normal PT and bleeding time)
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A: X-linked recessive, Factor VIII deficiency (most common severe bleeding disorder). Labs: prolonged aPTT (intrinsic pathway), normal PT, normal bleeding time/platelet count. Presentation: deep tissue bleeding (hemarthroses — knee most common, muscle hematomas, intracranial hemorrhage). NOT mucocutaneous (that's platelet disorders). Hemophilia B: Factor IX deficiency (Christmas disease). Treatment: Factor VIII replacement, desmopressin (mild cases — releases vWF/FVIII stores). Emicizumab: bispecific antibody for prophylaxis. Reference: Harrison — Hemophilia.

questions.questionText: Polycythemia vera (PV) is a myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by:
questions.optionB: JAK2 V617F mutation (>95%), elevated RBC mass, low EPO, and risk of thrombosis and transformation to myelofibrosis/AML
questions.explanation: PV: clonal stem cell disorder → primarily erythrocytosis. JAK2 V617F: present in >95% (diagnostic). Features: elevated Hb/Hct/RBC mass, LOW serum EPO (differentiates from secondary polycythemia — EPO high), splenomegaly, pruritus (especially after warm bathing — aquagenic, mast cell histamine), thrombosis (most common cause of morbidity), erythromelalgia. Treatment: phlebotomy (target Hct <45%), low-dose aspirin, hydroxyurea (high-risk). Risk of transformation to myelofibrosis or AML. Reference: Harrison — PV.
